entry.title: Implementing B2B Advisory in SAP Integration Suite for Custom XML EDIFACT Scenario
entry.content:
In many organizations, B2B transactions are not always handled using pure standard formats. Over time, custom XML structures are introduced on top of EDIFACT messages to align with internal processing or EDI broker requirements.

I encountered a similar scenario in one of my recent projects. Through our EDI broker, we were consuming EDIFACT messages in an XML representation. With the upcoming S/4HANA migration, the same integrations needed to be moved to SAP Integration Suite.

Interestingly, the migration itself was not the biggest challenge. The real concern was monitoring and trading partner mantinance.

In our existing SAP PI/PO landscape, we have more than 400 active interfaces processing approximately 200,000+ transactions. Monitoring in such a landscape is already complex. Moving to Integration Suite without a structured monitoring approach would only increase the operational overhead.

Why We Considered B2B Advisory

To address this, we evaluated B2B Advisory capabilities in Integration Suite. The key expectations were:

  • Automatic payload logging retention for a defined number of days
  • Clear identification of trading partner information
  • Agreement-based processing for structured partner management
  • A dedicated monitoring environment for B2B transactions

Another important aspect was our S/4HANA clean core strategy. By leveraging B2B Advisory features properly, we see opportunities to reduce custom logic and keep the core system less cluttered with monitoring-specific enhancements.

Migration Context: From PO Without TPM to Integration Suite
In our SAP PO system, we were not using Trading Partner Management (TPM). As a result, many partner-specific handling rules were built into custom logic. While setting up B2B Advisory in Integration Suite, we started seeing clear structural benefits — especially around agreement handling and partner-based message identification. Even at this in-progress stage, the difference in approach compared to PO is quite visible.

This blog focuses on how I configured B2B Advisory to support our XML-based custom EDIFACT messages. Since we could not directly use the standard packages as-is, I made specific adjustments to adapt them to our scenario. I will walk through:

  • How the standard B2B Advisory packages behave
  • What limitations we observed with custom XML EDIFACT
  • What modifications were required
  • Key learnings during configuration

This is still work in progress, but the insights gained so far are already valuable, especially for teams moving from PI/PO to Integration Suite without prior TPM usage.

  • I have devided thie blog into 3 parts where current blog refers the configuration what we need to do in TPM (Trading partner Management such as Compnay profile, Partner profile, Identifiers, Communication configuration etc.
  • 2nd blog will be based on --> agreements and couple of features i have used.
  • 3rd blog will be how Iflows are configured and customized as per the need.

Quick Concept: How B2B Advisory & Integration works

 Above is the a explanitary flow how messgae processing works with B2B Advisory. Here the brain is TPM Setup and Iflows are executing engine. Let's break down the setup I have created within TPM.

 

TPM Setup for B2B Advisory

As explained in the processing flow, the standard B2B Advisory packages rely heavily on Trading Partner Management (TPM). The iFlow itself does not contain hardcoded partner logic. Instead, it dynamically resolves sender, receiver, and agreement details from TPM configuration at runtime. The TPM setup was structured as follows.

Company

The Company object represents our organization within the B2B landscape. In our case:

  • It reflects our enterprise entity interacting with external partners
  • This acts as the anchor for all agreements.
  • All inbound and outbound B2B agreements are defined relative to this Company.

Important consideration:
We ensured that the company identifiers align with the values extracted from the XML payload. If the identifiers do not match exactly, agreement resolution will fail.

Here I have created 2 identifiers from SAP where 1st "BTPISEDI" is the one i'm using for all outbound from SAP (Idoc always) as this is the port we have defined for IDoc in WE21. and "SID_CLNT_CODE" for all inbound to SAP as that is the logical system for all inbound. How those will be used will be describe in coming setps.

On the continuation on Company profile, we have Identifer where we can add individual identifer and Group Identifier. Group will be used when we have the varite of identifers. 

IdentifierActual value from the payload which will be use to identify the flow
AliasRepresentation of the identifer which will be display at config level
Type SystemType System means the Source Document type in simple language
Scheme Name/CodeKind of the identifer number for internal use eg : Customer Number or Partner Profile
Agency Name/CodeKind of the identidier for the trading partner eg : DUNS number or Sender ID

Within the Trading Partner Management setup, the Systems tab allows us to define communication system details such as IDoc or Process Direct configuration parameters.

However, it is important to understand that these are not actual adapters.

The configurations maintained here do not represent runtime adapters directly. Instead, they act as logical placeholders that the standard B2B package references during execution.

How It Works Conceptually:

In TPM, we define a System for:

  • External partner side
  • Our backend system (e.g., S/4HANA)
  • Type System (System version)

Under the System definition, we maintain adapter-related attributes such as:

  • Endpoint url of the system (internet/On-Prim)
  • Location ID (if applicable)
  • Process Direct url
  • Credentails if application 

These settings are then picked up by the standard B2B iFlow at runtime. The iFlow reads the resolved Agreement --> identifies the associated Systems --> and dynamically maps the configured values into the actual adapter that is connected in the integration flow.

Learning from various hit/try : If under system purpose is maintain as test, then in case of inbound to SAP, Idoc will never post for automatic posting it should be marked at production.

Partner Profile

Next step would be maintain the system and trading partner information. Here 2 section are devided into 2 maintinance. 1st is the Communication Partner (EDI Broker/Trading Partner Connected System/any other middleware) and Trading Partner (your actual customer/vendor).

Quick concept on Communication Partner :

  • protocol supported Process Direct and AS2.
  • only connection details are requried under communication partner.
  • technical placeholder for connection.
  • certificates/signatature (for signing and encrption) are available (mostly used with AS2)

Trading Partner

Same setup what we do in company profile need to be recreate here. Except, we can ignore the communication part (channel details/type system). Just adding the reference of the communication partner is sufficient.

 

Here in the identifier for trading partner I have used group of identifiers instead of individual identifier (nothing intresting just because I have tried individual).  These Identifier are used to fetch the correct agreement which will have a better monitoring view per trading partner.

How to extract Identifiers

Ok, we have discussed about how to maintain identifer, but how to find the identfier the a little complex. 

  • Company Profile
    • Control Records from Idoc should be the first choice as that have all the Sender/Receiver information such as Logical system, Sender port, Receiver partner profile code etc. 
      • For Company Profile (Outbound) : As generally we have a single company profile so we should choose a value which is common accross all the Outbound flow (Logical system/XML Port are preferable)
      • For Company Profile (Inbound) : Preferable option is the Logical System which we pass in RCVPRN
  • Trading Partner Profile
    • Check the file which you will be consuming and the section where Sender/Receiver information is being transfer 
      StandardSegmentSender Position

      Receiver Position

      EDIFACTUNBS002 (4th element)

      S003 (5th element)

      ANSI X12ISAISA06ISA08
      VDA (Classic)511Pos 4-12Pos 13-21
      EDIWheelBuyer/Seller Party--
      Customized formatSender/Receiver section  

 

Next series of this blog will be on how agreements can be create (will not be too standard as I have faced couple of challange) and additional paramters which are useful. Also, the configuration we made in this part, how these play a valuable role and where these will be visible at runtime. 

link : Implementing B2B Advisory in SAP Integration Suite for Custom XML EDIFACT Scenarios part - 2

 

Thanks

Aman Sharma

entry.title: Achieve Event Driven Architecture and FIFO Approach with Google PubSub Adapter in Cloud Integration
entry.content:
With the growing popularity of Event Drive Architecture (EDA), message based adapters are a popular choice. Google PubSub Adapter for SAP Integration Suite is one such adapter. Understanding the architecture of the Google PubSub Subscription is a complex undertaking, therefore this blog attempts to simplify the same by creating a simple Integration Flow (iflow) to achieve Event Driven Architecture with FIFO approach.

Additionally majority of EDA based use cases revolve around the First In First Out (FIFO) Approach. Take E-Commerce for example. One would want that unless an order has been successfully processed there should not be any inventory change or the status of an order should not change to Shipped unless the payment has been completed. Such a crucial mechanism needs to be handled without any chance of error. Using Google PubSub with the necessary configurations, we can create such a scenario with ease.

Google PubSub Concepts

Before getting into the Integration flow, let's talk a little about the architecture of Google PubSub(GPS) itself.

GPS has three main components, a topic, the subscription attached to the topic, and the message that flows from topic to subscription. When a message is published to a topic, a messageId and publishTime is attached to it. Uniqueness of a message is decided by the assigned messageId.

Configuring Google PubSub Topic

Create a new topic and give it a unique ID. You could choose to have a default subscription attached to it. The ID of the default subscription would be decided by GPS itself (usually appending "-sub" after the Topic ID). Keep the rest of the configurations unchecked as they are not relevant to our use case.

Configuring Google PubSub Subscription

You can create a new subscription or work with the default subscription attached to the topic.

If you create a new subscription ensure that it is of Pull delivery type. This ensure subscribers to request for message using the Google PubSub Sender Adapter.

A Pull delivery type subscription has the following configurations.

The important configurations to create an FIFO Event Driven architecture are:

  • Exactly once delivery: Enable to ensure messages are not resent before the Acknowledgement Deadline expires.
  • Message ordering: Enable to ensure that messages with the same ordering key are received by the subscription in the order they were published.
  • Acknowledgement deadline (optional): Decides in how much time an acknowledgement is expected. If no acknowledgement is received within the expiration of this deadline, GPS topic is free to send the message to any of its subscriptions. You must set an acknowledgement deadline greater than the maximum integration flow processing time. This field technically does not affect the FIFO approach but setting a higher value is advantageous in terms of redelivery attempts.

Configuring Google PubSub Sender (REST)

Below is a basic iflow to read messages from a GPS Subscription. An exception subprocess block has been added to handle any error that occurs.

Ensure that the exception sub process ends with an Error End Event.

This is extremely important. The behavior of the Integration Suite and the adapter itself depends highly on these events. Error End Event tells the adapter that the business process flow encountered an error and a negative Message Processing Log (MPL) is created. This negative MPL is dealt with differently as compared to a positive MPL.

After configuring the Connection tab of the GPS Sender (for more info, see SAP Cloud Integration – Getting started with Google Pub/Sub Adapter), switch to Processing tab.

Project ID : ID assigned to your Google Cloud Console project.

Subscription ID: ID of the subscription from which messages will be read.

Max Messages to Read: Maximum number of messages to be read in a single poll. 

More details for these values which can be found here SAP Help.

Select Post-Processing as Acknowledge after Successful Processing; this ensures that the message is not acknowledged by the adapter, if the iflow processing ends with an error i.e. a negative MPL was created. Remember our previous explanation concerning Error End Event.

Following these recommendations and values will ensure an iflow with FIFO approach is successfully designed.

Sample Use Case 1 : Messages with same Ordering Key

The flow is as follows:

  1. Messages M1 and M2 are published to a GPS Topic with the same Ordering key. You can achieve this in a different iflow using the Google PubSub Receiver Adapter or via an external publisher system.
  2. The first message M1 is read by the GPS Sender attached to our iflow. Note that since M1 and M2 were part of the same ordering key and the subscription had Ordering Enabled, GPS does not allow M2 to be even sent before M1 is out of the Topic.
  3. The GPS Sender sends the message M1 to the iflow for processing.
  4. Lets say an error occurs while processing this message M1. The Exception Sub-Process is invoked, and after the necessary steps (as per your business logic; e.g.. create a JIRA ticket), iflow ends with an Error End Event.
  5. A negative MPL is created for the processing of M1. GPS Sender receives this negative MPL back and does not send an acknowledgement to the GPS Subscription.
  6. GPS Subscription does not receive an Acknowledgement for M1 within the set acknowledgement deadline. M1 remains in the topic. 
  7. Google PubSub Sender will not receive the second message M2 until M1 is removed from its topic either by acknowledging it or by moving it to a Dead Letter Queue using a different configuration or by manually removing it.

Sample Use Case 2: Messages with different Ordering Keys

Let us see a similar thing with two messages of same ordering key and another message with a different ordering key.

Messages M1 and M2 published with same ordering key 1

      

Message M3 with a different ordering key 3 published.

Iflow receives M1 and M3 repeatedly until they are successfully processed by the iflow. M2 was not received as its predecessor M1 was not processed completely and was not removed from the topic.

  

 

Enabled Ordering Key in the Subscription ensures that M1 is processed before M2 as they have the same ordering key and M1 was published before M2.

Enabled Exactly Once Delivery ensure M1 is not resent until Acknowledgement Deadline expiration occurs which in this case was 120 seconds.

Conclusion

By leveraging the Google PubSub Adapter in SAP Integration Suite we are able to achieve strict message ordering which is a must if you are focusing on the integrity of an Event Driven Business Model. You can avoid a scenario where "Update" event comes before "Create" event in a database call.

In short Event Driven Architecture means your system stays updated in real-time, FIFO ensures those updates happen in the correct sequence, and Google PubSub Adapter ensures Event Driven Architecture is implemented with FIFO.

Happy Integrating!

entry.title: SAP CI to ABAP Integration: Seamless XML to Internal Table Mapping
entry.content:
In many SAP CI integrations, XML responses are received from external systems and passed to the ABAP layer. A common requirement is to convert this XML data into an internal table in ABAP.

There are two common approaches:

  1. Use XSLT mapping in SAP Backend (SAP ECC/S4HANA) to transform the XML into the required structure. ( You can follow blog https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-and-automation-blog-posts/xslt-transformations-xml-2-abap-a-working-example-with-deep-structures/ba-p/13370654 )
  2. Prepare the XML in SAP CI in ABAP XML format so that it can be directly converted into an internal table using ABAP transformation.

This blog explains the second and simpler approach.

Why Use ABAP XML Format?

If the XML follows the ABAP XML structure with the required tags:

  • <abap version="1.0">
  • <values>

Then ABAP can directly deserialize the XML into an internal table using:

CALL TRANSFORMATION id

This avoids complex XSLT mappings and simplifies the integration.

Sample XML That Can Be Directly Converted

Below is a sample XML payload prepared in SAP CI that can be directly converted into an internal table in ABAP:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<abap version="1.0">
    <values>
        <OUTPUT>
            <ZCON_PAC_LOCATION>
                <LOCATIONCODE>US</LOCATIONCODE>
                <PXCREATEDATETIME>2010-01-29 15:44:01</PXCREATEDATETIME>
                <PXCREATEOPNAME>Fname Lname</PXCREATEOPNAME>
                <PXCREATEOPERATOR>test@mail.com</PXCREATEOPERATOR>
            </ZCON_PAC_LOCATION>
        </OUTPUT>
    </values>
</abap>

Important: The tags <abap version="1.0"> and <values> are mandatory for ABAP deserialization using the ID transformation.

Generating the XSD

The simplest way to generate the XSD is:

  1. Create an RFC-enabled function module with the required ABAP structure.
  2. Access the WSDL using: http://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/soap/wsdl11?services=<RFC_Name>&sap-client=<client>
  3. Copy the relevant schema portion from the WSDL.

Sample XSD

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xs:schema xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
           xmlns:asx="http://www.sap.com/abapxml"
           xmlns:sap="http://www.sap.com/sapxsl"
           elementFormDefault="qualified"
           attributeFormDefault="unqualified">

    <xs:element name="abap" type="AbapType"/>

    <xs:complexType name="AbapType">
        <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="values" type="ValuesType"/>
        </xs:sequence>
        <xs:attribute name="version" type="xs:string" use="required"/>
    </xs:complexType>

    <xs:complexType name="ValuesType">
        <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="OUTPUT" type="OutputType"/>
        </xs:sequence>
    </xs:complexType>

    <xs:complexType name="OutputType">
        <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="ZCN_PAC_LOCATION"
                        type="PacLocation"
                        minOccurs="0"
                        maxOccurs="unbounded"/>
        </xs:sequence>
    </xs:complexType>

    <xs:complexType name="PacLocation">
        <xs:sequence>
            <xs:element name="LOCATIONCODE" type="xs:string" minOccurs="0"/>
            <xs:element name="PXCREATEDATETIME" type="xs:string" minOccurs="0"/>
            <xs:element name="PXCREATEOPNAME" type="xs:string" minOccurs="0"/>
            <xs:element name="PXCREATEOPERATOR" type="xs:string" minOccurs="0"/>
        </xs:sequence>
    </xs:complexType>
</xs:schema>

SAP CI Mapping:


In SAP CI, map the response so that:

  • The root node is abap with attribute version="1.0".
  • Under it, include values.
  • Under values, include your wrapper node such as OUTPUT.
  • Inside the wrapper, repeat the structure node for each record.

Once structured correctly, no additional transformation is required in ABAP.

ABAP code :

"Data Declarations
DATA: lo_http_client TYPE REF TO if_http_client,
      lv_request     TYPE xstring,
      lv_response    TYPE xstring.
"Convert Internal Table to XML (Request to CPI)
CALL TRANSFORMATION id
  SOURCE pac_project_department_request = lt_request[]
  RESULT XML lv_request.

"Create HTTP Client
cl_http_client=>create_by_destination(
    EXPORTING
        destination              = 'DestinationName'
    IMPORTING
        client                   = lo_http_client
    EXCEPTIONS
        argument_not_found       = 1
        destination_not_found    = 2
        destination_no_authority = 3
        plugin_not_active        = 4
        internal_error           = 5
        OTHERS                   = 6 ).

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
"Suitable error handling
ENDIF.
"Disable logon popup:
lo_http_client->propertytype_logon_popup = lo_http_client->co_disabled.
"Send HTTP Request
lo_http_client->request->set_method( 'POST' ).
"Set xml request body
lo_http_client->request->set_data( data = lv_request ).
lo_http_client->send(
    EXCEPTIONS
        http_communication_failure = 1
        http_invalid_state         = 2
        http_processing_failed     = 3
        http_invalid_timeout       = 4
        OTHERS                     = 5 ).

IF sy-subrc = 0.
    lo_http_client->receive(
        EXCEPTIONS
            http_communication_failure = 1
            http_invalid_state         = 2
            http_processing_failed     = 3
            OTHERS                     = 5 ).
ENDIF.
lo_http_client->close( ).
lv_response = lo_http_client->response->get_data( ).
"Convert XML Response to Internal Table
CALL TRANSFORMATION id
  SOURCE XML lv_response
  RESULT output = et_pac_departments.

ABAP Dictionary Objects

  • Internal table: ET_PAC_DEPARTMENTS
  • Table type: ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT_TY
  • Line type: ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT

The internal table ET_PAC_DEPARTMENTS is defined with table type ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT_TY.

The table type ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT_TY refers to the line structure ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT.

The structure ZCON_PAC_DEPARTMENT contains the following fields:

  • LOCATIONCODE (TYPE string)
  • PXCREATEDATETIME (TYPE string)
  • PXCREATEOPNAME (TYPE string)
  • PXCREATEOPERATOR (TYPE string)

Since the XML structure matches the ABAP structure exactly, the CALL TRANSFORMATION id statement automatically deserializes the XML and populates the internal table.

 

Conclusion

By preparing the XML payload in SAP CI using ABAP XML format with the correct structure and mandatory tags, we can directly convert the XML into an internal table in ABAP using CALL TRANSFORMATION id.

This approach simplifies SAP CI integration scenarios and reduces transformation complexity significantly.

entry.title: Implementing B2B Advisory in SAP Integration Suite for Custom XML EDIFACT Scenario Part - 2
entry.content:
In Part 1, I explained the overall setup of B2B Advisory. How the TPM foundation was structured. With the Company, Trading Partner, and Systems configuration in place, the next critical layer is the Agreement.

In B2B Advisory, the Agreement is not just a configuration object — it is the runtime decision engine. Every inbound or outbound message must resolve to a valid Agreement. If it does not, the processing stops at the monitoring layer.

In this part, I will focus on how Agreement determination works how it can be create, how it interacts with payload identifiers, and what challenges arise when dealing with XML-based EDIFACT.

What is Agreement

In simple language, an Agreement in B2B Advisory defines the rules under which a message is allowed to be processed between two parties. It answers four basic questions:

  • Who is sending the message?
  • Who is receiving the message?
  • What type of message is it?
  • In which direction is it flowing (Inbound or Outbound)?

If all these conditions match, the system allows the message to proceed. If not, processing stops.

You can think of an Agreement as a contract between two systems. Unless a valid contract exists, the message cannot move forward.

Agreement Templates

Agreement template are created for the refernce of an actual agreement. It consist a desgin flow of how actual end-2-end B2B flow works. Who it the party (Our business or Trading partner), what all systems are involve, communication adapters information what messgae are we using, invlolved identifer. This is just a template where all the input values can be changed later on while creating an actual agreement.

Agreement

This is the core of the B2B Advisory.  While creating an agreement, you can take refernce of an existing agreement template so that a desing flow with the connected systems wil automatically populated.

Working with copy with template --> agreement will copy the existing configuration available in selected template and give the option to modify as requried. (preferable choice)

Working with bind with template --> agreement will bind with the existing configruation and doesnot allow to change configuration. 

Agreement Overview

This include the overview of your agreement, who is the initiator, connected type system, SAP system and what are the identifers involve to check wether the incoming messgae have one of these identifer. These identifer are not entered manually instead is take refernce from the profile (Company/Trading profile). You have a option to create on prompt but advisable is to take refernce from the profile (Single/Group Identifer).

Learning from agreement
  • While creating an agreement sometime a confusion arise that, for one trading partner if we have multiple types of business transactions, then should we create multiple agreements. No, we have options to create multiple business transaction scenario in one agreement (inbound and outbound both). Don't treat an agreement as one interface, agreement is something a contract between your orgnization and trading partner. So while create an agreement name it as per the involve trading partner.
  • For custom formats there is not option I have found for the Custom type system, So I decided to use type system as "SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc" for Trading Partner (considered after various failed hit and try). As based on the Type system standard iFlow behaves differntly and most suitable to work with the XML "SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc" is best as per my R&D.
  • We have options to create custom MIG & MAG under custom type system but there is no options I found to include those in the agreement. So I Have used standard MIG of each messgae for my Integration.
  • While fetching the standard MIGs message version are available but there is no guarantee the SAP version you have will be available. In my case for ORDERS.ORDER05 have two versions "S4HANA 1709–Version - S4HANA 1709" and "1809_FPS02–Version - 1809_FPS02" and for DESADV.DELVRY07 I see only "1809_FPS02–Version - 1809_FPS02". Where as in our landscape we have "S/4HANA 2023". 
  • Key consideration is choosing different version on the IDoc may create issue, as the metadata available in yours SAP will not be the same available in the MIG you selected. So instead of choosing MIG in you mapping design, we refer the structure what is available in the SAP. Also, MIG selection is mandatory in the agreement and agreement is based on the Type System, so ultimatly in the Type system (Company/Trading partner profile') mentioned version what you have in you SAP and what is available in you MIG.
    S/4HANA Version (MIG)ABAP/Basis Release (IDoc)
    1709752
    1809753
    1909754
    2020755
    2023758

 
B2B Scenario

Next step will be creating the transactions all inbounds and outbounds with the selected trading partner, the actual business flow.

Here we have the designed flow which have the prebuild steps as:

Sender & Receiver can be your SAP system or Trading partner system depends on the what you have choose on the transaction create screen where Interchange have the message and involve type system version information (version/MIG can be editied manually).

Other features what we have here are the Activity Paramters, Custom Search Attributes and Custom Rules. 

Activity Parameter -->These can be used for additional mapping logic. for example storing partner profile code, logical system deatils, port details, DUNS number, EDI messgae version, other hardcoded values per trading partner. These can be manually create or can be extend from Company/Trading parter profile. In short partner specific values can be maintained here.

Custom Search Value --> To get more filters for the monitoring such as any messgae id, plant, ship-to, buyer-id etc. These can be added with the help of parameters, headers and XPath.

Transaction Configuration

After these configuration we have to start mantain the channels, mapping, MIG version etc. In the Source Communication we need to maintain the channle, these are not actual channle/adapter instead a template which we create in Company/Trading profile. Actual adapter which will pick the file, need to develop in cloud integration (IFlow designer) which will execute these agreement transaction.

In the inerchnage mentioned the actual MIG used, Number range object, and the version of your SAP system. There are certain other options also we have in case those are requrired as per your requirement. As in my case I have the customized XML of EDIFact file I have used the MIG which is available for me for the business process I have and in the SAP Version I have chaged from original MIG to the one which we have in our landscape. I have used the same process for both sender/receiver interchange.

Mapping Logic --> We have the option to execute the mapping developed in Cloud Integration via process direct or we can use the MAG whatever suite the requirement. In my case because of custom logics I am haved process direct which is calling a seperate iflow developed in cloud Integation.

Once everythig is in there place after activating the agreement, all the information present till agreement will be executed in the partner directory data, which is the runtime enviournment for execution.

Here the Activity Plain ID the the matching string, once that is found correct agreement will be executed else the that transaction will fail with the error "Agreement Configuration Access Failed". 

What This Plan ID Template Represents --> The Agreement Plan ID is constructed by combining multiple message and partner attributes into a single structured string.

  • sndAdapterType --> The communication adapter (Process_Direct, AS2, IDoc, etc.)
  • sndDocumentStandard --> EDIFACT, X12, SAP_IDoc etc.
  • sndMessageNamespace --> Namespace maintained in design
  • sndMessageVersion -->  Version like D96A, 758 etc.
  • dynamic internal placeholder --> %s
  • sndSenderIdQualifier -->  Sender ID Identifier (single/group)
  • sndSenderPartnerType -->  Type of partner (Customer, Vendor)
  • sndSenderSystemId -->  Sender system identifier
  • sndSenderSystemIdQualifier -->  Qualifier for sender system
  • dynamic internal placeholder --> %s
  • sndReceiverIdQualifier -->  Receiver qualifier
  • sndReceiverPartnerType -->  Receiver partner type
  • sndReceiverSystemId -->  Receiver system
  • sndReceiverSystemIdQualifier -->  Receiver qualifier
  • sndMessageType -->  Message type like ORDERS
sndAdapterType + "-" + sndDocumentStandard + "-" + sndMessageNamespace + "-" + sndMessageVersion + "-%s-" + sndSenderIdQualifier + "-" + sndSenderPartnerType + "-" + sndSenderSystemId + "-" + sndSenderSystemIdQualifier + "-%s-" + sndReceiverIdQualifier + "-" + sndReceiverPartnerType + "-" + sndReceiverSystemId + "-" + sndReceiverSystemIdQualifier + "-" + sndMessageType

This is how an end-2-end configuration of agreement works. In the next step I will show how standard package behaves with this agreement and what changes I made in one of the flow for inbound custom XML based EDIFact.

link (will be update once published) : Implementing B2B Advisory in SAP Integration Suite for Custom XML EDIFACT Scenarios part - 3

 

Thanks

Aman Sharma

entry.title: At-Least-Once Delivery (ALOD) in SAP Commerce: A Practical Guide
entry.content:
Overview

When we build integrations in SAP Commerce Cloud, especially for important business data like orders, reliability becomes very important. We cannot afford to lose messages just because an external system was temporarily down.

This is where the At-Least-Once Delivery (ALOD) feature really helps.

What is ALOD?

ALOD stands for At-Least-Once Delivery.

As the name says, it guarantees that outbound webhook events are delivered at least once. If a webhook call fails due to network issues or the external system being unavailable, SAP Commerce automatically retries sending the message for up to 24 hours. If the message still cannot be delivered after multiple attempts, it is moved to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) so that we can monitor and investigate it later.

So instead of losing data, we always have visibility and control.

Why Is This Important?

Let’s take a simple example. Suppose you need to send order data to an external system. In a traditional approach:

  • You write custom code to call the external API.
  • You implement retry logic manually.
  • If retries fail, the data is lost.
  • You only have logs to check what happened.

This approach works, but it adds complexity and risk.

With ALOD:

  • You publish an event instead of directly calling the external system.
  • The webhook listens to the event.
  • If delivery fails, the system retries automatically.
  • If it still fails, the message goes to the DLQ.

This makes the whole integration more reliable and easier to manage.

How ALOD Works in SAP Commerce Cloud

In SAP Commerce Cloud, ALOD works together with webhooks and Azure Queue. When ALOD is enabled for a webhook:

  • The event payload is stored in an Azure Queue.
  • The system processes it asynchronously.
  • If delivery fails, it retries automatically.
  • If undeliverable, it moves the message to a Dead Letter Queue.

For every ALOD-enabled webhook, a corresponding DLQ container is also created.

This gives us:

  • Automatic retry mechanism
  • Safe storage of failed messages
  • Ability to download and analyze undelivered messages

High-Level Implementation Steps

  1. Add Required Extensions
    • webhookservices
    • webhookbackoffice
  2. Assign Required Roles

    To access integration screens and DLQ monitoring in Backoffice, users need:

    • backofficeadministratorrole
    • deadletterqueuemonitoringgroup.     
    •  
  3. Create a Custom Business Event

    Create a custom event, for example CustomOrderExportValueEvent, and make sure it extends AbstractEvent so that it is available when generating the model class. This event should contain all the payload data that needs to be sent to the external endpoint. The POJO structure should be mapped using the standard approach through impex or API configuration.

        
  4. Configure Webhook and Enable ALOD

    Create a WebhookConfiguration and make sure the ALOD flag is enabled.     

  5. Trigger the Event

    Instead of calling the external system directly inside your order process:

    • Trigger the custom event.
    • The webhook configuration listens to it.
    • The message gets stored in Azure Queue.
    • Processing happens asynchronously.  

Important Points to Remember

1) Payload Size Limitation

  • Default payload limit in standard code: 20 KB
  • Azure Queue maximum limit: 64 KB
  • Payload size validation happens in AzureEventPersistentQueueService
  • If you need a bigger payload, you should customize carefully

2) It Is Asynchronous

ALOD processing is completely asynchronous. Your main business process does not wait for the external system response.

3) Adding Custom Headers

If you need to add extra headers to webhook requests, you can implement OutboundRequestDecorator. This helps you add custom key-value headers before the request is sent.

Final Thoughts

From my experience, ALOD is a very useful feature when dealing with important outbound integrations. Instead of writing complex retry logic and worrying about data loss, we can rely on the platform’s built-in eventing and queue mechanism.

It makes integrations:

  • More reliable
  • Easier to monitor
  • Cleaner in design
  • Less risky

If you are working on outbound integrations in SAP Commerce Cloud, enabling ALOD is definitely something you should consider.

Useful References

  • Webhooks Documentation
  • POJO Integration Objects
  • At-Least-Once Delivery
  • Creating a Webhook with a POJO Integration Object
  • Advanced Integration Object Modeling
  • Modifying Outbound Request

entry.title: SAP BTP – GDCR/DDCR - Domain-Driven-Centric-Router on APIM & CPI (1.49M Validated Requests)
entry.content:
Introduction

Enterprise API landscapes are traditionally organized around vendors and backend systems.
However, business processes are domain-centric by nature.

Gateway Domain-Centric Routing (GDCR) proposes a vendor-agnostic architectural model where APIs are exposed per business domain and backend resolution is handled dynamically through deterministic, metadata-driven routing.

The implementation repository referenced in the DOI contains artifacts specifically focused on the SAP BTP Integration Suite, particularly:

  • SAP API Management (referred to as DCRP)
  • SAP Cloud Integration (referred to as PDCP)

At the SAP CPI level, the “domain-centric” alignment applies strictly to structural naming conventions and governance organization — including:

  • Technical users
  • Packages
  • iFlow DNA structure
  • Value Mapping entries
  • Script collections (global or per domain)

It does not introduce additional complexity to iFlow development itself. If interpreted as an attempt to increase development complexity, the proposal is being misunderstood.

The CPI layer remains technically conventional; the innovation lies in semantic organization and metadata-driven routing governance — not in altering integration development fundamentals.

At the SAP API Management level, the model is more sophisticated and requires practical APIM experience. However, after reading the DOI and understanding the deterministic routing lifecycle, the provided documentation and artifacts should enable an architect with API Management.

The repository provides structural implementation support.
The DOI provides the architectural reasoning required to understand and reproduce the model correctly.

A Vendor-Agnostic Metadata-Driven Architecture for Enterprise API Governance

Enterprise API landscapes are typically organized around systems and vendors

  • Salesforce APIs
  • SAP APIs
  • AWS APIs
  • Azure APIs

However, business processes are not vendor-centric. They are domain-centric:

  • Order-to-Cash
  • Procure-to-Pay
  • Record-to-Report

Gateway Domain-Centric Routing (GDCR) proposes a different model:

Framework Components

  • GDCR – Gateway Domain-Centric Routing
  • DDCR – Domain Driven-Centric Routing
  • PDCP – Package Domain-Centric Pattern (SAP CPI alignment name conventions)
  • DCRP –  Domain Centric-Routing Pattern ( SAP APIM )

Expose one proxy per business domain, and resolve backend systems dynamically through semantic metadata.

No proprietary, confidential, or actual production customer data was ever accessed or used at any time during the research efforts described in this work. All validations were executed on trial/sandbox environments.

Core Concept

GDCR separates:

  • Business intent (domain / entity / action)
  • Technical execution (endpoint / adapter / protocol)

Inbound URLs such as:

/sales/orders/create/salesforce

are normalized into canonical routing keys, validated against a metadata control plane (KVM), and dynamically bound to backend endpoints.mProxies remain immutable.mBackend systems can change without impacting consumers.

The DDCR Engine

At the heart of the framework is the Domain-Driven Centric Router (DDCR) — a deterministic seven-stage lifecycle:

  • Parse
  • Normalize
  • Canonicalize
  • Validate (Route Guard)
  • Lookup (Metadata resolution)
  • Bind (Dynamic target resolution)
  • Execute

Security is enforced through:

  • Canonical normalization of action space
  • Whitelist validation before lookup
  • Fail-fast rejection (HTTP 500) for unregistered combinations
  • Backend URLs never derived from client input

The engine operates as a Layer-7 semantic control plane, analogous to control/data plane separation in SDN and service mesh architectures.

Novelty and positioning

DDCR does not introduce isolated new primitives.
Instead, it formalizes a deterministic composition of established integration principles into a prescriptive execution model for enterprise API governance.

The contribution lies in defining a runtime-executable semantic routing grammar with a fixed seven-stage lifecycle invariant, independent of vendor platform.

This lifecycle binds:

  • Canonical domain-semantic normalization
  • Explicit whitelist validation (Route Guard)
  • Metadata-driven dynamic backend resolution
  • Immutable gateway façades
  • Into a single deterministic execution chain.

While metadata-driven routing, domain-oriented API structuring, and control/data-plane separation concepts exist in prior integration paradigms, GDCR/DDCR articulates them as a unified Layer-7 Semantic Control Plane with strict lifecycle guarantees and fail-fast safety constraints.

The innovation is therefore architectural formalization and deterministic execution invariance — not individual technical mechanisms in isolation.By separating business intent (domain / entity / action) from technical execution (endpoint / adapter / protocol), the framework enables vendor interchangeability, backend evolution, and governance stability without consumer impact.

— Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana March 02, 2026

Cross-Platform Validation (February 2026)

The model was validated across:

  • SAP BTP (API Management + Cloud Integration)
  • Kong (Docker)
  • AWS + Cloud Integration
  • Microsoft Azure + Cloud Integration

Total execution:

  • 1,499,869 requests
  • 100% routing success (zero routing failures)
  • 99.9916% end-to-end success
  • All failures classified as trial/sandbox network interruptions (ECONNRESET / ETIMEDOUT)

All validation was performed exclusively in trial/sandbox environments. No production or confidential data was accessed.

Newman JavaScript Policy - Phantom v12:

On SAP BTP specifically, the DDCR Phantom v12 engine processed 121,471 requests through API Management and Cloud Integration (4 domains, 44 vendors, 4 proxies, 44 iFlows) with 99.9967% success and a cross‑Atlantic latency of

p50 145 ms, p85 184 ms, p99 338 ms — all with immutable proxies and routing policies.

Architectural Trade-Off: Determinism vs Encoding

Routing metadata is intentionally human-readable:

gdcrpordersusalesforceemeaid02:http

A strict O(1) binary encoding could increase lookup speed marginally, but at the cost of operational transparency and auditability.

🚫- 0x9F3A12BC → http
or
🚫- 110010101011011001 → adapter_07

Blazing fast.
Completely unreadable.
Operationally hostile.

Engineering quality does not depend on infrastructure. It depends on the engine — and the engineer’s brain 🧠.

Current routing resolution averages ~2 ms while preserving:

  • Human inspection
  • Zero external tooling dependency
  • Deterministic fail-fast behavior

After reviewing the DOI documentation and deploying the provided artifacts, architects with API Management experience should be able to execute an initial PoC within roughly 10–30 minutes.

 

“Architecture is not about what a system can do. It is about what the business needs it to be  —  enabled by correct architectural engineering.” 

Mandatory Documentation & Implementation Guidance

Downloading and reading the DOI document is mandatory before attempting any implementation.

The document contains:

  • 12 pages of architectural explanation
  • 15 pages of complementary diagrams, routing matrices, and structural tables

The GitHub repository is referenced inside the DOI document.

Accessing the repository without first reading the DOI will have little practical value and may lead to misinterpretation.

Github - In the folder:

/gdcr-proven/gdcr-dcrp-sap-api/files-repository

You will find:

  • 4 SAP APIM proxies
  • 44 static routing mappings - 10 business processes structured by domain
  • Newman collection
  • Newman execution smoke and 200k+ messages
  • Postman Collection
  • 4 CPI packages
  • 44 iFlow
  • Need to setup user and password for APIM and CPI

To execute the Postman collection:

  • Replace the host
  • Replace username and password credentials

Important

The Key Value Mapping (KVM) is not exportable and must be created manually. It is strongly recommended to keep the provided naming conventions.nIf names are changed, the policy logic must be adjusted accordingly.

Example KVM:

cpipackage-nx.sales.o2c.integrations | cpipackage-nx.finance.r2r.integrations ...


GDCR is an architectural framework — not a configuration shortcut.

Note:

The internal routing references used during validation were environment-specific and will not function externally without proper configuration.

Due to platform constraints, certain Key-Value Mapping (KVM) strings were not exportable.

The deterministic routing grammar and metadata construction logic required to rebuild the KVM entries are fully explained in the DOI.

Without understanding:

  • Domain normalization logic
  • Routing identifier construction
  • Metadata abstraction model
  • Deterministic resolution sequence

it is not possible to correctly reconstruct the routing configuration.

The repository provides structure and files for plugin and play SAP APIM and CPI but The DOI provides the logic - The DOI must be read first.

License, Attribution & Publication Record

© 2026 Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana

This publication establishes a public prior publication record of the GDCR/DDCR framework as of February 7, 2026 (initial DOI release).

This work is released under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) License.

You are free to use, copy, reproduce, modify, distribute, and build upon this framework for any purpose, including commercial applications, provided that appropriate attribution is given to the original author and reference to the DOI is maintained.

Full formal documentation:

DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18582492

🛡️ Semantic Architecture. Deterministic Governance. Vendor Independence.

Enterprise Integration Architect
SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert all capabilities
SAP Press e-Bite Author — Enterprise Messaging (2021)

#GDCR #DDCR #EnterpriseArchitecture #APIGovernance #SAPBTPIntegrationSuite #SAPAPIM #SAPCPI #OpenSource #IntegrationPatterns #VendorAgnostic #Microservices

entry.title: Modelling IBP-to-IBP Integration Flows with SAP Integration Suite – Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Hello Community,

 

Introduction

Over the past years, CI-DS has been widely used to model integration interfaces with SAP IBP as both source and target system with or without an intermediate step via storing data in a file on server, covering use cases such as:

  • Housecleaning – Marking obsolete master data to alter clean within IBP. While S/4HANA's integration add-on can cleanse its own staging tables, this doesn't cascade automatically to IBP.
  • Rule-based attribute value definition – The Manage Rules for Master Data Maintenance app in IBP has limitations, for complex cases the integration framework is usually the way to go.
  • Data copies with transformations – Handling cases where the standard IBP Copy operator falls short: copying a key figure with a period offset, duplicating data from one planning object to multiple target planning objects, etc.
  • Version and planning area fan-out – Sending master data or key figures from one version (or planning area) to multiple versions (or planning areas), including data transformations.
  • Complex key figure calculations not yet simplified by the IBP configuration layer.
  • Master data attributes as KF reloads – Initializing time buckets at the end of the rolling time horizon.

Since IBP release 2508, the Harmonised Planning Area (HPA) has introduced a unified structure supporting both tactical and operational planning, combining time-series and order-based data. Alongside this major innovation, the integration layer is also evolving: as highlighted in a previous blog entry, the integration framework for IBP is shifting toward a combination of RTI and CI (the Cloud Integration capability of the Integration Suite service on BTP).

This blog entry continues the series on highlighting how to model IBP-to-IBP integration flows using Cloud Integration.

 

How to Model IBP-to-IBP Flows in Cloud Integration

Integration Suite offers the flexibility to cover these scenarios — and many others — within a single, configurable integration flow.

The starting point is the "SAP IBP – Reusable Integration Flows Examples" package. This package provides two sets of grouped reusable iFlows : first to read and extract data from a source IBP destination, another to write data to the to the same or a different IBP destination. Before building your own flows, I strongly recommend consulting the Integration guide for SAP IBP reusable integration flows examples.

One way to model this end to end is shown below as an overview:

 

📥 Step 1 – Read Data from IBP

To read data from IBP, call the following reusable iFlows in sequence, using RequestReply building blocks with a ProcessDirect adapter:

#

iFlow

Purpose

1

SAP_IBP_Read_-_Initialize

Opens a server-side read session on the IBP OData service, allocating resources to hold query context, pagination state, and result set.

2

SAP_IBP_Read_-_Fetch_Data

Fetches data pages from IBP's OData service, handling pagination automatically until all records have been extracted.

3

SAP_IBP_Read_-_Close

Closes the read session opened by Initialize. You can call this at the end of your flow — including after the write steps — to release server resources.

⚠️ Note: the Close iFlow is intentionally called last, after data transformation and all write steps have completed.

 

🔄 Step 2 – Transform Data

Once the XML payload has been fetched from IBP, you can apply transformations using the Message Mapping building block. This is where IBP-to-IBP flows deliver real value: transforming or adjusting data in ways that would be difficult or impossible to achieve directly within IBP.

Example: Housecleaning via a Deletion Indicator

Consider a scenario where S/4HANA regularly sends products to IBP based on an integration add-on filter (e.g., MRP type = X0). If a product is later made non-IBP-relevant, the add-on can stop sending it — but because data is loaded in insert/update mode, the product record remains in IBP master data.

A clean solution: use a timestamp attribute that tracks the last update date for each master data record. In your Message Mapping, you can evaluate this date and — if the record hasn't been updated since yesterday — mark it with an X in a Deletion Indicator attribute. The logic relies on the fact that master data is loaded daily: a record not updated today was not sent by S/4HANA and is therefore no longer integration-relevant.

Here's how to build this mapping once you've added it in your flow:

Load your source and target XSD schemas (an example is attached to this blog entry — rename the file extension from .xml to .xsd). You can use two different schema files for source and target, adapting each to include only the fields you're reading from or writing back to IBP. An XML editor like Notepad++ works well for these adjustments.

 

Once you've loaded both schemas, you can use the drag and drop method to link your attributes together 1:1.
For our example, focusing on the deletion indicator mechanism we will link the changed on date attribute (defined as timestamp in IBP) with the Product Deletion Indicator attribute (defined as nvarchar in IBP).

In the Mapping Expression panel, chain the following functions:

  1. substring – Trim the timestamp to YYYY-MM-DD format (10 characters)
  2. compareDates – Compare the result against currentDate, specifying the expected date format for both inputs
  3. equals(number) – Check the output of compareDates:
    • 0 → dates are equal (record updated today)
    • -1 → changed-on date is earlier than today
    • 1 → changed-on date is later than today
  4. If – If the result is -1, set the Deletion Indicator to X; otherwise leave it blank

 

🔧 Combining the Transformed Payload with Write Parameters

After the Message Mapping, your message carries transformed IBP master data. Before calling the write iFlows, you need to merge this payload with the 'Write' configuration parameters (batch name, IBP destination, master data type, etc.) passed via a Content Modifier step.

To do this cleanly:

   Filter the Message

Add a Filter building block immediately after the Message Mapping step.

  • Set the XPath expression to: //item

This strips the outer XML hierarchy (header node, <IBPReadMasterDataPRODUCT> wrapper) and retains only the <item> nodes carrying the actual master data values.

sidenote : to see this payload info above you need to enable Trace logs and deploy your whole iflow. see here how to enable these detailed logs.

   Save the Payload as an Exchange Property

Add a Content Modifier step. Create an exchange property with:

Field

Value

Name

payload (or any name you prefer)

Source Type

Expression

Source Value

${body}

Data Type

java.lang.String

Exchange properties persist throughout the entire flow and can be referenced at any later stage — this is the key to combining the two messages.

 

📤 Step 3 – Write Data to IBP

With the transformed data saved as an exchange property, you're ready to write back to IBP. Call the following reusable iFlows in sequence, again via RequestReply + ProcessDirect, as highlighted in the Integration guide for SAP IBP reusable integration flows examples.

#

iFlow

Purpose

1

SAP_IBP_Write_-_Create_Batches

Creates a batch header (name, ID, operation type: insert/update, replace, or delete) with no data yet. The batch is already visible in IBP's Data Integration app at this point.

2

SAP_IBP_Write_-_Post_Data

Creates batch items and loads master data or key figure records to the IBP staging tables.

3

SAP_IBP_Write_-_Process_Posted_Data

Triggers IBP's post-processing step to move data from staging tables to application tables, making it visible in planning.

You need two Content Modifier steps to supply the required parameters:

Before Create_Batches: Provide the batch key, batch name, command, and IBP destination. This step is already mentioned above as it also references your saved exchange property to carry the master data payload forward.

Before Post_Data: Provide the batch ID, master data type/prefix, and selected attributes. To inject the previously saved payload into the message body, simply reference the exchange property directly: ${property.payload}

💡 Flexibility at the Write Step

Because the IBP destination is a configurable parameter in the Content Modifier, it's straightforward to:

  • Send data to a different IBP tenant (e.g., read from Production, write to Test)
  • Send data to a different version, using the PlanningAreaVersion parameter (for master data types that support version-specific MD)
  • Send data to a different master data type (e.g., from pre-prod Product MD to quality Product MD) by adjusting the MasterDataPrefix / MasterDataType parameters

 

🔒 Step 4 – Close the Read Session

Always end your flow by calling SAP_IBP_Read_-_Close. This releases the server-side read session opened by Initialize and frees up resources on the IBP backend.

 

Summary

Initialize → Fetch_Data → [Message Mapping + Filter + Save Property] → Create_Batches → Post_Data → Process_Posted_Data → Close

This pattern gives you a single, configurable integration flow that can cover a wide range of IBP-to-IBP scenarios — housecleaning, data copies, version fan-out, cross-tenant refreshes — simply by adjusting parameters in your Content Modifier steps and adapting the Message Mapping logic.

I hope this walkthrough gives you a solid foundation for building your own IBP-to-IBP flows with Cloud Integration. Feel free to share questions or your own variations in the comments below!

entry.title: IBP Integration with SAP Integration Suite – Your One-Stop Reference
entry.content:
👋 Welcome

This blog entry is a living index of resources covering SAP IBP integration using SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration). Whether you are just getting started or looking for guidance on a specific scenario, this is the place to find the right articles.

The goal of this entry is simple: as the series grows — with new posts from myself and from fellow community contributors — this page will be updated to reflect the latest content, so you always have a single reference point.

💡 Bookmark this page and check back regularly. It will be updated as new blog entries are published.

 

📚 Blog Series Index

 

🔷 Foundation & Setup

These entries cover the fundamentals: understanding why Cloud Integration is relevant for IBP and how to get your environment configured.

Blog Entry Description
Enhancing SAP IBP Integration – Evolution and InnovationBroader perspective on the evolution of the IBP integration landscape, covering both current capabilities and the direction of innovation. A good read for understanding the strategic context.
SAP IBP – New Integration Option: Introducing SAP Cloud Integration from BTPOverview of Cloud Integration as a new integration option for IBP. The starting point for the series — explains the shift in integration strategy and what CI brings to the table.

🔷 S/4HANA (private cloud or on-premise) to IBP Integration

Hands-on guidance for creating integration flows based on SAP delivered reusable content, focusing on S/4HANA private/on-prem as source or target.

Blog Entry Description
Configuring SAP Cloud Integration for an IBP-to-S/4HANA On-Premise (Private Cloud) scenarioStep-by-step configuration guide for connecting Cloud Integration to an S/4HANA on-premise or private cloud system from IBP. Covers connectivity setup, credentials, and key configuration parameters.
Creating Your First iFlow in Cloud Integration for an IBP / S/4HANA On-Prem scenarioA practical walkthrough for building your first integration flow between IBP and S/4HANA on-premise, using the reusable iFlow building blocks available in the IBP package.
IBP Integration Using SAP Cloud Integration – Fetch Product MD from Add-OnPractical guide to fetching Product master data from the S/4HANA integration add-on and loading it into IBP via Cloud Integration.

🔷 IBP-to-IBP Integration

Scenarios where IBP is both source and target — for data copies, transformations, housecleaning, and more.

Blog Entry Description
Modelling IBP-to-IBP Integration Flows with Cloud IntegrationHow to model a full IBP-to-IBP flow using the reusable iFlows: reading data with Initialize / Fetch / Close, transforming it via Message Mapping (including a housecleaning example), and writing it back using Create Batches / Post Data / Process Posted Data.

🔷 File-Based Integration

Covers integration scenarios involving file exchange via SFTP.

Blog Entry Description
IBP File-Based Integration with SFTP Server via Integration Suite – Cloud IntegrationHow to implement file-based integration for IBP using an SFTP server and Cloud Integration, including flow design, adapter configuration, and file handling patterns.

 

Want to Contribute?

If you have written or are planning a blog entry on IBP integration with Integration Suite, feel free to leave a comment below with your link and a short description. I will review it and add it to the index.

SAP Integrated Business Planning Cloud Integration SAP BTP ABAP environment SAP S/4HANA SAP Integration Suite

entry.title: 9 ways to create Interface Fact Sheets in SAP LeanIX
entry.content:
Interface Fact Sheets in LeanIX can explain the data flow between two applications on a logical and on a technical level. A logical interface would be "Replicate BusinessPartner Create from SAP S/4HANA Finance to SalesForce". A technical interface might be one specific integration component (e.g. Integration Flow on SAP Integration Suite, Cloud Integration or the API Proxy in front of SalesForce on API Management) which can be only a part of the logical interface.

 

The criteria to differentiate the integration options is 

  • level of automation
  • granularity (logical and/or technical interface)
  • which data sources are supported

Here´s the list:

 

Option

Automation & Granularity

Supported Data Sources

1. Manual Entry 

manual (logical)

n/a – no integration used

2. Bulk Import

semi-automated (technical)

n/a – no integration used

3. GraphQL API

automated (logical/technical)

MAKE: whatever you program

4. LeanIX Adapter

automated (logical/technical)

MAKE: whatever you program

5. Standard Content: NTT Data

automated (logical only)

SAP IS: Integration Assessment

6. Standard Content: RealCoreautomated (technical only)SAP IS: Cloud Integration
SAP IS: API Management
SAP Process Orchestration
7. SAP Cloud ALMsemi-automated (technical only)Monitored SAP-Systems

8. LeanIX MCP Server

automated (logical/technical)

Create not possible yet, potentially various data sources in future

9. WHINT Integration Cockpit

automated (logical & technical)

Multiple: SAP and Non-SAP Integration Layer Components

You can find a more detailed explanation in the Integration Excellence blog: https://www.integration-excellence.com/how-to-feed-sap-leanix-with-interface-fact-sheets/

entry.title: The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To.
entry.content:
Somewhere in your organisation, there is an integration landscape that is costing you more than it should, moving slower than the business demands, and carrying more risk than anyone has fully quantified. It probably spans more than one middleware platform, perhaps several. It almost certainly lacks complete documentation. And the team maintaining it is spending the majority of its time keeping the lights on rather than delivering anything new.

This is not a niche problem. It is the default state of enterprise integration in 2025. Decades of organic growth, acquisitions, departmental autonomy, and pragmatic decisions made under project pressure have produced landscapes of extraordinary complexity and extraordinary cost. Ask any CIO how many integration platforms are actively running in their organisation and the answer is rarely one. More typically it is three, four, or five: SAP PI/PO handling core SAP connectivity, MuleSoft managing API integrations, a legacy webMethods instance still processing B2B traffic, Boomi picked up through an acquisition, and perhaps a remnant BizTalk deployment that nobody has found the budget to retire.

The Multi-Platform Problem: Why Fragmentation Costs More Than Anyone Realises

Running multiple integration platforms simultaneously is rarely a deliberate strategic choice. It is almost always the accumulated result of individual decisions that each seemed reasonable at the time. But the cumulative effect, technically and commercially is severe, and it compounds with every passing year that the landscape is left in its fragmented state.

The Technical Cost of Fragmentation

Each platform in a multi-middleware landscape brings its own runtime, its own design-time tooling, its own monitoring stack, its own error handling patterns, and its own deployment and release process. The consequence is a set of technical problems that do not appear on any single platform’s licence invoice but are very real in their impact on the integration team and on the business it serves.

  • No single pane of glass : When an end-to-end business process fails, an order that never made it to the warehouse, a payment that did not reach the finance system, the operations team must investigate across multiple monitoring tools, each with different log formats, different retention windows, and different levels of observability. What should be a five-minute investigation becomes a 90-minute cross-platform triage exercise.
  • Incompatible security and governance models : Credential management, access policies, encryption standards, and transport protocols are defined separately on every platform. Applying a new security policy consistently across a fragmented landscape requires parallel effort on every system and creates gaps where inconsistencies go undetected. Audit and compliance exercises that should surface clean evidence instead expose a patchwork of different controls applied to different degrees.
  • Tribal knowledge and platform silos : Deep expertise on any one middleware platform takes years to build. An organisation running four platforms must maintain four pools of specialist knowledge, often held by individuals rather than teams. The departure of one expert can leave entire parts of the integration landscape effectively unsupported. And because interfaces on different platforms were built by different teams using different patterns, cross-platform consistency in design standards, error handling, and documentation is rarely achieved.
  • No coherent high-availability story : Failover, disaster recovery, and planned maintenance management must be solved separately for every platform. Each has different failover mechanisms, different RPO and RTO characteristics, and different operational runbooks. The result is an integration estate where resilience is patchy and inconsistent - high for some interfaces, untested or absent for others.

The Commercial Cost of Fragmentation

The financial consequences of a fragmented integration landscape are rarely visible in a single budget line which is precisely why they persist. They are distributed across licence costs, infrastructure costs, personnel costs, and the operational overhead of managing complexity. When Tarento models the full Total Cost of Ownership for a typical multi-platform landscape, the numbers are consistently eye-opening.

  • Duplicate licence and support costs : Each middleware platform carries its own annual licence, its own vendor support contract, and its own renewal negotiation cycle. Organisations paying MuleSoft licence fees while also holding SAP PI/PO maintenance contracts and already covered for SAP Integration Suite under their RISE or GROW subscription are in the uncomfortable position of paying three times for integration capability they could consolidate onto one platform. Tarento’s ROI Calculator consistently shows annual licence and support savings of hundreds of thousands to millions of pounds once consolidation is modelled against the full current-state cost base.
  • Multiplied infrastructure and operations overhead : On-premise and hybrid middleware platforms require infrastructure to run: servers, patching cycles, DR environments, and the engineering time to maintain them. Cloud platforms require their own tenants, their own environment management, and their own upgrade planning. Multiplied across three, four, or five platforms, the infrastructure and operational overhead is a significant and recurring cost that consolidation can largely eliminate.
  • The hidden cost of slower delivery : New integration requirements, connecting a new SaaS application, enabling a new supplier, automating a new business process must be scoped and delivered separately on whichever platform happens to own that domain. Routing decisions are made not by strategic logic but by which team has capacity. Reusable assets built on one platform cannot be leveraged on another. The net effect is that a fragmented landscape systematically slows down the pace at which the business can evolve its digital operations, with a real but difficult-to-quantify opportunity cost attached to every delayed integration project.

 

The Multi-Platform Tax: Every integration platform an organisation runs above one adds licence cost, infrastructure overhead, operational complexity, and staffing expense. Tarento’s TCO modelling of multi-platform landscapes consistently shows that the annual cost of running three or more middleware platforms, when all cost components are properly accounted for is 2 to 4 times higher than the equivalent cost on a single, consolidated cloud-native platform. This is the multi-platform tax. And most organisations have been paying it for years without fully recognising it.

 

 

Why Leading Enterprises Are Consolidating Now

The multi-platform problem is not new. What has changed is that the pressure to solve it has become acute at exactly the same moment that a credible consolidation target has emerged and the tools to execute the migration have matured to the point where the economics are compelling.

Global enterprise leaders, from Nordic consumer goods conglomerates and global port equipment manufacturers to Asian diversified conglomerates and global specialty chemicals companies, are executing integration consolidation programmes not because it is strategically tidy, but because the business case is undeniable. Reduce the licence estate. Eliminate the infrastructure overhead. Build a single, expert integration team rather than four specialist silos. Adopt a unified monitoring and governance model. And position the integration layer to support the AI-driven business transformation agenda that every board is now being held accountable for delivering.

SAP Integration Suite on BTP has become the consolidation target of choice for SAP-centric enterprises. It is not simply that it is included in RISE and GROW subscriptions, though that matters significantly for the financial case. It is that the platform genuinely delivers the breadth of integration capability that was previously split across multiple tools: A2A, B2B/EDI, API management, event-driven integration through SAP Event Mesh, and pre-built connectivity to thousands of applications through the integration content catalogue. Consolidating onto SAP Integration Suite does not require the business to accept reduced capability. In most cases, it expands it.

The question is no longer whether to consolidate. The question is how to do it without the budget spiralling, the timeline extending indefinitely, and the business being disrupted in the process. That is exactly the question that Tarento has been answering, in production, for some of the world’s most demanding enterprises.

We have migrated landscapes spanning 150 to more than 2,500+ interfaces. We have consolidated platforms ranging from SAP PI/PO and MuleSoft to webMethods, Boomi, BizTalk, Informatica and others -  all onto SAP Integration Suite. And we have done it faster, cheaper, and with lower risk than any purely manual approach could achieve. The reason is iVolve: Tarento’s AI-powered integration modernisation programme, built from the ground up around automation at every stage of the journey.

Today, we are launching a five-part blog series that takes you inside the iVolve approach; from the strategic decisions that set a programme up for success, to the automation tools that make the numbers work, to the operational capabilities that keep a modernised landscape running at enterprise grade. Whether you are a CTO building the case for a board that wants to see ROI, an integration architect scoping a migration you know needs to happen, or an IT operations leader looking for optimisations, this series is written for you.

 

 

The State of Enterprise Integration in 2026

Enterprise integration is at an inflection point. Three forces are converging to make the status quo untenable for most large organisations.

Force 1: The End-of-Support Clock

SAP PI/PO mainstream maintenance ends in 2027. SAP CPI Neo is being deprecated in favour of Cloud Foundry. webMethods is mid-transition following its acquisition by IBM. MuleSoft licensing costs continue to escalate. BizTalk has been in extended support for years. For organisations on any of these platforms, the migration question is not a strategic preference, it is a deadline. Every quarter of inaction is a quarter closer to running unsupported, unlicensed, or unviable software at the heart of business operations.

Force 2: The Economics of Cloud-Native Integration

SAP Integration Suite on BTP is included as part of most SAP RISE and GROW subscriptions. Organisations standardising on S/4HANA, SAP Cloud, or the broader SAP BTP ecosystem are already paying for world-class integration capability, and leaving it unused while continuing to pay separate licences for legacy middleware. The TCO differential, when properly modelled, is striking. Tarento’s customers have seen financial projections showing returns of 200% to 350% over ten years, with break-even periods as short as 12 to 18 months. The economics of staying put are simply no longer defensible.

Force 3: The Velocity Imperative

Business units are no longer willing to wait 12 weeks for a new integration to be delivered by an overwhelmed integration team managing 500 legacy interfaces. The AI-driven business transformation programmes that CEOs are committing to require an integration layer that can move at the pace of the business; real-time, event-driven, API-enabled, and capable of connecting new SaaS applications in days rather than months. Legacy middleware, by design, cannot deliver this. Cloud-native integration platforms, properly implemented and operated, can.

An Emerging Context : AI and Integration

The iPaaS market is experiencing explosive growth, projected to reach $79.38 billion by 2030, driven by AI's transformative impact on integration platforms. AI is revolutionizing iPaaS by enabling intelligent automation, self-healing infrastructure, and proactive monitoring, shifting platforms from reactive data movers to intelligent hubs that predict failures and optimize workflows.

Leading vendors like Boomi, Workato, Informatica, and SAP are rapidly integrating AI-powered assistants and low-code/no-code capabilities to democratize integration development, allowing citizen developers to build automation without deep technical expertise. A critical driver is real-time data access: agentic AI applications demand seamless, context-aware data retrieval across enterprise systems, making AI-powered integration platforms essential for next-generation AI workflows.

However, organizations face emerging security challenges, by 2028, 25% of enterprise breaches may stem from AI agent abuse, making governance and built-in guardrails paramount. The shift from "SaaS with AI features" to "AI-native SaaS" in 2026 signals that platforms embedding AI natively from inception will dominate, while legacy integration approaches become obsolete in supporting modern AI-driven digital transformation.

SAP leads the 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for iPaaS (fifth consecutive year) with generative AI capabilities enabling workflow automation, API anomaly detection, and intelligent data mapping. The platform offers 10,500+ pre-built artifacts and 250+ third-party adapters, with 95% customer recommendation rates. SAP positions Integration Suite as the critical infrastructure for agentic AI applications requiring secure, real-time API access across enterprise systems.

However, there continues to be an ongoing debate about SAP's strength primarily being with existing SAP customers seeking to consolidate iPaaS and connect SAP with non-SAP systems, rather than as a standalone iPaaS competitor. Personally, I feel that SAP has closed the gap significantly and is now a serious contender, with an added advantage of having the edge when it comes to operating in a SAP centric ecosystem.

 

Why Migration Programmes Fail, and Why iVolve Is Different

Integration migration projects have a poor reputation for good reason. They routinely overrun timelines, exhaust budgets, and fail to deliver the operational improvements that justified the investment. The pattern is familiar: an optimistic initial estimate that did not account for the full complexity of the landscape, a manual migration process that scales badly, a testing phase that consumes months because test cases had to be written from scratch, and a post-go-live environment that lacks the governance and tooling to be properly operated.

iVolve was purpose-built to break this pattern. Tarento is an SAP Validated Partner Use Case holder and an SAP Migration Factory Partner. iVolve is available on the SAP Store. The iVolve Framework replaces the manual, error-prone, effort-intensive stages of a migration with automation, AI, and proven accelerators achieving automation factors of 30% to 95% depending on the source platform, compressing programme timelines from years to months, and delivering a post-migration landscape that is actively governed, monitored, and resilient from day one.

 

What’s in the Series

Over five blogs, we cover every stage of the integration modernisation journey, the various iVolve tools and accelerators including approaches that make each stage faster, more certain, and more valuable.

 

Part A

The Integration Modernisation Imperative

The strategic case for integration modernisation, why SAP Integration Suite is the right target platform, and how the iVolve lifecycle framework addresses every stage from legacy discovery to operational excellence.

Part B

Making the Case

How to build a compelling, finance-grade business case for SAP Integration Suite consolidation including TCO modelling, NPV, IRR, and the ROI Calculator tool that has helped enterprises from global manufacturers to diversified conglomerates unlock investment approval.

Part C

Automate Everything

A deep dive into the iVolve automation engine; the Discovery Agent, Migration Automator, the MINT App for Neo-to-Cloud Foundry migrations, and the TestEase Bundle with real programme benchmarks showing automation factors of 30% to 95% across every major source platform.

Part D

Beyond Go-Live

How the iVolve OpEase Suite; transport governance, enhanced monitoring, AI-powered alerts, and the iFlow AI Advisory App ensures a modernised SAP Integration Suite landscape operates with the rigour and intelligence that enterprise operations demand.

Part E

When Downtime Is Not an Option

Why organisations that run real-time business operations on SAP Integration Suite need a proven failover strategy, and how the OneFailover Application delivers near-zero downtime through automated sync, orchestrated cutover, and continuous readiness scoring.

 

Who Should Read This Series

These blogs are written for the leaders and practitioners who are closest to the integration challenge, and the ones who will be held accountable for solving it.

  • CTOs and IT leadership who need to make a defensible case for integration modernisation investment and want the financial framework and customer evidence to do it
  • Enterprise and solution architects who are planning a migration, want to understand the automation tooling in depth, and need to scope a programme that will actually deliver on its estimates
  • Integration operations teams who are managing a live SAP Integration Suite landscape and want to understand the tools available to improve resilience, governance, and operational intelligence
  • Finance and procurement leaders who will be asked to approve a migration investment and want to understand the financial model, the risk profile, and the track record behind the proposal
